coredata.dc:description: Effective immune homeostasis modulation and re-epithelialization promotion are crucial for accelerating burn wound healing. Cell migration is fundamental to re-epithelialization, with epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) as a key mechanism. A sustained inflammatory environment or impaired macrophage transition to M2 phenotype can hinder pro-resolving cytokine activation, further delaying the recruitment, migration, and re-epithelialization of epidermal cells to the injury site, ultimately compromising wound healing. Herein, the bioactive flavonol quercetin is transformed into pharmacologically active carbonized polymer dots (Qu-CDs) spray with high water dispersibility, permeability and biocompatibility for full-thickness skin burns treatment. Qu-CDs spray can efficiently initiate macrophage reprogramming and promote the transition of macrophages from M1 to M2 phenotype, modulating immune responses and facilitating the shift from the inflammatory phase to re-epithelialization. Additionally, Qu-CDs spray can promote cell migration and re-epithelialization of wound edge epithelial cells by inducing an EMT process without growth factors, further accelerating the reconstruction of the normal epidermal barrier. Mechanistically, Qu-CDs spray activates the smad1/5 signaling pathway for promoting the EMT phenotype of wound edge epithelial cells. Overall, this study facilitates the construction of novel spray dosage form of pharmacologically active carbonized polymer dots with desired bioactivities for effective wound healing.

Activation of smad1/5 signaling contributes to EMT and cell motility induced by Qu-CDs
The Qu-CDs facilitate full-thickness cutaneous burn wound healing in vivo

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of design and preparation of Qu-CDs for the treatment of burn wound healing. (a) Qu-CDs can accelerate the transition of wounds to the repair phase by modulating immune responses, and reinforce wound re-epithelialization by actively inducing the EMT phenotype via the smad1/5 signaling pathway, ultimately facilitating the reconstruction of the normal epidermal barrier and achieving rapid wound closure. (b) Typical TEM images of Qu-CDs.

Characterization of Qu-CDs. (a) UV–Vis spectrum of Qu-CDs. Inset: digital photograph of the Qu-CDs dispersed in water. (b) FT-IR spectra of the precursor quercetin, as well as the Qu-CDs. Raman spectrum (c) and PXRD pattern (d) of Qu-CDs. (e) XPS survey spectra of quercetin and Qu-CDs. (f) High-resolution N 1s XPS spectra of quercetin and Qu-CDs. (g) High-resolution C 1s XPS spectra of quercetin and Qu-CDs. (h) High-resolution O 1s XPS spectra of quercetin and Qu-CDs. (i) TGA curves of quercetin and Qu-CDs.

Biocompatibility of Qu-CDs and Quercetin. Cytotoxicity of Qu-CDs (a) and quercetin (b) towards HaCaT cells after incubating for 24 h. (c) Flow cytometry analysis for apoptosis assay of HaCaT cells treated with Qu-CDs at different concentrations. (d) EdU-staining images of Qu-CDs and quercetin on cellular proliferation of HaCaT cells. (e) H&E staining images of major organs in the burn mice after various treatments on day 21. EGF: epidermal growth factor. (f) Quantitative analysis of the EdU-positive cells. (g) The body weight variation of the burn mice (n = 6). Data are presented as mean ± SD. n = 3, ∗∗p < 0.01; ∗∗∗p < 0.001; ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001.

Immunomodulatory effect of Qu-CDs and quercetin in vitro. (a) Schematic illustration of the transition of macrophages from the pro-inflammatory M1 to the anti-inflammatory M2 phenotype. (b) Relative mRNA expression of anti-inflammatory cytokines including IL-10 and Arg-1 in Raw264.7 cells. The value of blank control groups was calibrated to 1. Raw264.7 cells are treated with LPS (100 ng mL−1) for 24 h. (c) Relative mRNA expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β in Raw264.7 cells. The value of blank control groups was calibrated to 1. Raw264.7 cells are treated with LPS (100 ng mL−1) for 24 h. (d, e) Immunofluorescence staining images of the expression of iNOS and CD206 in macrophages. (f, g) Quantification of iNOS-positive and CD206-positive cells in various experimental groups. Data are presented as mean ± SD. n = 3, ∗∗p < 0.01; ∗∗∗p < 0.001; ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001.

Effects of Qu-CDs and quercetin on cell migration and EMT induction. (a) Cell migration of HaCaT cells treated with Qu-CDs and quercetin is detected by the scratch assay. Relative mRNA expression (b) and proteins expression (c) associated with EMT: E-cadherin, N-cadherin, vimentin, fibronectin and cytokeratin. HaCaT cells are treated with various concentrations of Qu-CDs and quercetin for 24 h. (d) Semiquantitative analysis of the expression level of specific proteins in western blot analysis. (e–h) Immunofluorescence images of E-cadherin, N-cadherin, vimentin, and cytokeratin in HaCaT cells in various experimental groups. Data are presented as mean ± SD. n = 3, ∗∗p < 0.01; ∗∗∗p < 0.001; ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001; ns, not significant.

Activation of smad1/5 signaling contributes to EMT and cell motility induced by Qu-CDs. (a) Volcano plot of RNA-seq data from HaCaT cells treated with Qu-CDs for 24 h. Each point represents an individual gene and all genes differentially expressed in control or Qu-CDs samples with a p < 0.05 and folds change >1.5 are highlighted in red (up-regulated) or blue (down-regulated). The grey points are genes that have no differentially expressed. (b) The thirty most significant Gene Ontology terms from differentially expressed genes (DEG). (c) KEGG analysis of signaling pathway enrichment. (d) Relative gene expression levels of smad1, smad5, ID1, ID2, ID3, and ID4 when smad1/5 is blocked or not. (e) Assessment of EMT genes expression in HaCaT cells exposure to Qu-CDs when smad1/5 is blocked or not. Western blot analysis (f) and semiquantitative analysis (g) of expression of smad1/5, p-smad1/5, E-cad, and N-cad in HaCaT cells. (h) Immunofluorescence staining images of smad1/5, p-smad1/5, E-cad, and N-cad in HaCaT cells exposed to Qu-CDs with or without inhibitor of smad1/5 signaling pathway. (i) Cell migration of HaCaT cells exposed to Qu-CDs with or without inhibiting the smad1/5 signaling pathway is detected by scratch assay. Abbreviations: "+" and "−" denote "with" and "without", respectively. Data are presented as mean ± SD. ∗p < 0.05; ∗∗p < 0.01; ∗∗∗p < 0.001; ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The Qu-CDs facilitate full-thickness cutaneous burn wound healing in vivo. (a) Schematic illustration demonstrating the construction of a burn injury model. (b, c) Repair of mouse skin burn wounds. (d) H&E staining images of migration zone and center of skin burns. Blue arrows: hair follicles and sebaceous glands. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The anti-inflammatory and EMT induction effects of Qu-CDs in vivo. (a) Immunofluorescence staining images of TNF-α and IL-1β on days 3 and 7 post-surgery. (b) Immunohistochemical staining images of E-cad and N-cad on days 7 and 14 post-surgery. (c) The corresponding quantification analysis of the E-cad and N-cad on days 7 and 14 post-surgery in immunohistochemical staining samples. (d) Immunohistochemical staining images of p-smad1/5 on days 3, 7, and 14 post-surgery. (e) Mechanisms of accelerating wound healing by Qu-CDs. Qu-CDs suppress inflammation and reinforce wound re-epithelialization by actively inducing the EMT process and epithelial cell migration via the smad1/5 signaling pathway, ultimately facilitating the reconstruction of the normal epidermal barrier and achieving rapid wound closure. Data are presented as mean ± SD. n = 3, ∗∗p < 0.01; ∗∗∗p < 0.001; ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Effective immune homeostasis modulation and re-epithelialization promotion are crucial for accelerating burn wound healing. Cell migration is fundamental to re-epithelialization, with epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) as a key mechanism. A sustained inflammatory environment or impaired macrophage transition to M2 phenotype can hinder pro-resolving cytokine activation, further delaying the recruitment, migration, and re-epithelialization of epidermal cells to the injury site, ultimately compromising wound healing. Herein, the bioactive flavonol quercetin is transformed into pharmacologically active carbonized polymer dots (Qu-CDs) spray with high water dispersibility, permeability and biocompatibility for full-thickness skin burns treatment. Qu-CDs spray can efficiently initiate macrophage reprogramming and promote the transition of macrophages from M1 to M2 phenotype, modulating immune responses and facilitating the shift from the inflammatory phase to re-epithelialization. Additionally, Qu-CDs spray can promote cell migration and re-epithelialization of wound edge epithelial cells by inducing an EMT process without growth factors, further accelerating the reconstruction of the normal epidermal barrier. Mechanistically, Qu-CDs spray activates the smad1/5 signaling pathway for promoting the EMT phenotype of wound edge epithelial cells. Overall, this study facilitates the construction of novel spray dosage form of pharmacologically active carbonized polymer dots with desired bioactivities for effective wound healing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Burn injuries are the fourth leading cause of skin trauma, typically caused by physical, chemical, or radiation exposure, which can damage skin layers, subcutaneous tissues, and even internal organs in severe cases [1–3]. Severe burns may trigger systemic immune homeostasis disruption and complications like multiple organ failure [4,5]. Additionally, adjacent tissues can progress to necrosis, worsening injury depth and delaying healing [6,7]. Currently widely adopted clinical treatments face inherent limitations, including poor permeability and the need for frequent dressing changes, especially for wounds with significant exudate. These factors may inadvertently damage nascent tissues and cause discomfort or pain. Therefore, the efficient, safe, and comfortable clinical interventions are required to be developed urgently.

Wound healing encompasses four overlapping stages: hemostasis, inflammation, proliferation, and remodeling. Healing from burn injuries follows these pathways, where key biological events significantly contribute to skin repair [8,9]. Macrophages play a pivotal role in regulating immune-inflammatory responses in the microenvironment during the inflammatory phase, transitioning from the pro-inflammatory M1 phenotype to the M2 phenotype as the healing process advances to the proliferative phase [10]. M1 phenotype macrophages are capable of phagocytosing pathogens and tissue debris during the early stages of injury, while concurrently secreting various signaling molecules to orchestrate downstream events in the proliferative phase [11]. A sustained inflammatory environment or impaired transition of macrophage to M2 phenotype can hinder pro-resolving cytokine activation, further delaying the recruitment, migration, and re-epithelialization of epidermal cells to the injury site, ultimately compromising wound healing [12–16]. Concurrently, keratinocytes at the wound edges and within hair follicles undergo epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT), a pivotal driver of re-epithelialization in the tissue proliferation phase of wound healing. Re-epithelialization refers to the continuous process in which new epidermis gradually covers the wound surface, progressing from the margins towards the center, with differentiated epithelial cells regenerating and reconstructing above the dermal-epidermal junction (DEJ) [17]. Epithelial cells migrate collectively during this process, coordinating with various cells involved in tissue repair to restore the integrity of the damaged tissue [18,19]. EMT represents a typical model in cell migration, where keratinocytes transition through dedifferentiation, reorganization of adhesion molecules, and adoption of a mesenchymal phenotype [20–23]. This loosening of adhesion facilitates cellular migration across the wound surface, directly influencing the progression of re-epithelialization during wound repair [24,25]. Thus, both effective immune response modulation and re-epithelialization promotion are crucial therapeutic targets for accelerating the healing of injured tissues.

Current growth factors-based therapeutic strategies exhibit substantial promise, efficiently triggering cascading responses and accelerating epidermal regeneration [26,27]. However, their clinical efficacy is limited due to the unstable physicochemical properties and the potential risk of inducing excessive fibrosis [3]. Contemporary research largely focuses on employing advanced nano-therapy to encapsulate various growth factors, aiming to enhance their accumulation at the lesion site and prolong their effectiveness at the wound sites [28]. Nevertheless, before the introduction of these synthetic nanomaterials into clinical practice, challenges such as the ambiguous biocompatibility and degradation profiles, as well as the difficulties in large-scale production must be addressed [29]. Hence, pursuing novel, safe, manufacturable, and stable nanoformulations as alternatives to growth factors for modulating local immune homeostasis and inducing EMT represents a particularly appealing improvement strategy.

Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), a novel type of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials, are gaining attention in biomedical applications due to their high biocompatibility, ease of preparation, and advantages of widespread sources [30]. Previous studies have demonstrated their antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties in tissue regeneration [31]. Particularly, the precursor-dependent structural diversities of the surface functional groups endow CPDs with versatile functionalities [32–36]. Recent studies have shown that edible or medicinal plant-derived CPDs, such as water-soluble CPDs from onion skin powder and ginger water, can facilitate skin wound healing through potent anti-inflammatory activity or by promoting fibroblast migration [37,38]. Nowadays, natural extracts from sustainable sources, owing to their unique biological activities, have been extensively developed and applied in wound repair [39–42]. Flavonoid extracts, derived from fruits, tea leaves, seeds, or plant roots, are widely used as dietary supplements due to their excellent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties [43–45]. Among these, the flavonol quercetin is noted for its anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and tissue regenerative effects [46,47]. Flavonoids represent one of the most appealing and promising natural product families for treating skin issues [45]. However, the practical usage of quercetin is suffered from low water solubility and cytotoxicity [48]. In the present study, the quercetin is transformed into carbonized polymer dots (Qu-CDs) spray with high water dispersibility, permeability, and biocompatibility for full-thickness skin burns treatment. These well-designed Qu-CDs can initiate macrophage reprogramming, modulating immune responses by facilitating the transition of macrophages from M1 to M2 phenotype, thus accelerating the shift from the inflammatory phase to re-epithelialization. Notably, Qu-CDs can effectively promote cell migration and re-epithelialization at the wound edge by inducing an EMT phenotype without any growth factors, aiding in reconstructing the normal epidermal barrier. Mechanistically, Qu-CDs promote epithelial cells to initiate EMT phenotype via activating the smad1/5 signaling pathway. These sprayable Qu-CDs provide a new paradigm to develop bioactive CPDs with desired pharmacological activity to address the practical needs in burn wound healing (Fig. 1 a).

The carbonized polymer dots from quercetin (Qu-CDs) were synthesized via an ammonia-assisted hydrothermal process at 200 °C for 4 h. Typical transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images indicated that these Qu-CDs possessed an average diameter of 2.7 nm (Fig. 1b and Fig. S1). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrum demonstrated that Qu-CDs exhibited a broad absorption, with an absorption peak at ca. 280 nm that could be attributed to the π-π∗ transition of the aromatic π system in Qu-CDs (Fig. 2 a). The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) image in Fig. 2b revealed that most of the characteristic functional groups of the precursor quercetin were effectively retained in Qu-CDs. Raman spectrum of Qu-CDs as shown in Fig. 2c consisted of the D band as well as the G band, demonstrating the formation of the graphitic carbon cores in Qu-CDs during the carbonization process, which could also be verified by the presence of the characteristic (002) diffraction in the powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) pattern of Qu-CDs (Fig. 2d). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was then used to explore the distributions of functional groups on Qu-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 2e and f, the appearance of the N species in the XPS survey spectrum as well as the high-resolution N 1s XPS spectrum of the Qu-CDs could be attributed to the ammonia-induced formation of the N-doped graphitic carbon cores. As shown in Fig. 2g, Fig S2, and Fig S3, high-resolution C 1s XPS spectra revealed that the oxygen-containing species in the quercetin precursor were mainly C-O species and C=O species. By contrast, Qu-CDs were covered with C-O species, C=O species, and O-C=O species, which could be attributed to the occurrence of oxidation caused by the hydrothermal process in the presence of O2 and H2O at high temperature (200 °C). As demonstrated in Fig. 2h, Fig S4 and Fig S5, high-resolution O 1s XPS spectra also indicated the formation of O-C=O species on Qu-CDs. Notably, these O-C=O species provided Qu-CDs with increased dispersibility in aqueous condition (Fig. 2a). The successful construction of Qu-CDs could be additionally demonstrated by the changes in the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and derivative thermogravimetric analysis (DTG) as shown in Fig. 2i and Fig. S6, respectively. Moreover, the distributions of the molecular weight of Qu-CDs were in ca. 800–4000 as detected by matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF MS) as shown in Fig. S7. All these exciting results indicated that the quercetin precursor had gone through a process of polymerization and carbonization during the formation of the graphitic carbon cores, covered with abundant preserved functional moieties of the quercetin precursor. In other words, the present Qu-CDs enabled the transformation of the pharmacologic moieties in quercetin precursor to the nanoscale. The stability of Qu-CDs in solution was then investigated. As shown in Figs. S8–S10, after dispersing in aqueous condition for 60 days, no detectable structural change could be observed in the UV–Vis spectrum, FT-IR spectrum, and Raman spectrum, indicating the high stability of Qu-CDs. To effectively increase the dispersibility and biostability of Qu-CDs for further biological uses, Qu-CDs were functionalized with polyethylene glycol (PEG) via the interaction between the terminal phospholipid groups of mPEG-DSPE and the graphitic carbon structure in Qu-CDs. As shown in Figs. S11–S13, the ζ potential value of PEGylated Qu-CDs showed a significant increase, indicating the successful PEGylation of Qu-CDs. The digital photograph in Fig. S14 indicated that PEGylated Qu-CDs exhibited high dispersibility in water, saline, and Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) containing 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS). As depicted in Fig. S15, PEGylated Qu-CDs can be sprayed in an even, mist-like form from a spray bottle. Fig. S16 demonstrated that PEGylated Qu-CDs had significantly improved skin penetration compared to quercetin, achieving near-complete absorption in a shorter time. This enhanced permeability could be attributed to their nanoscale size and excellent water dispersibility, highlighting their potential to promote skin absorption and increase bioavailability. For convenience, the PEGylated Qu-CDs were also termed Qu-CDs in the following biological study.

As a potential enhancer of wound healing, the biocompatibility of Qu-CDs requires thorough assessment prior to future research applications. The biocompatibility of Qu-CDs and quercetin was evaluated using the cell counting kit 8 (CCK-8) assay. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, when the concentration of Qu-CDs was below 125 μg mL−1, the viability of HaCaT cells was comparable to the control group, indicating the ultrahigh biocompatibility of Qu-CDs (0 μg mL−1 to 125 μg mL−1). Notably, with the addition of a high concentration of Qu-CDs (250 μg mL−1), the cell viability significantly decreased to 74 % (p < 0.001). In contrast, the viability of HaCaT cells co-cultured with quercetin remained unaffected up to 5 μg mL−1 but decreased at 10 μg mL−1 (Fig. 3b). This result was consistent with previous studies, indicating a relatively narrow safe concentration range when quercetin was added alone, with the potential cytotoxicity even at lower concentrations [49]. Therefore, subsequent experiments used 5 μg/mL of quercetin, considering its cytotoxicity. Fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) results confirmed the excellent biocompatibility of Qu-CDs at concentrations 0–125 μg mL⁻1 (Fig. 3c). Besides, the 5-ethynyl-20-deoxyuridine (EdU) incorporation assay showed no significant difference in cell proliferation between Qu-CDs (125 μg mL⁻1) and quercetin (5 μg mL⁻1) treated groups (Fig. 3d and f). Additionally, in vivo biocompatibility was assessed using a burn wound mouse model, and revealed comparable body weight increase and no pathological changes in major organs with Qu-CDs treatment (Fig. 3e and g). Collectively, these findings supported the overall safety and feasibility of Qu-CDs in subsequent experiments.

The destructive effects of burns involve the disruption of a healthy skin barrier, which impairs immune system regulation and increases susceptibility to infections. Macrophages play a crucial role in regulating immune inflammation during wound healing, promoting cell proliferation and tissue recovery through various mechanisms such as phagocytosis, initiation and resolution of inflammation, and secretion of growth factors [50]. Macrophages display unique functional phenotypes that, through polarization in response to microenvironmental stimuli and signals, can either promote or inhibit the inflammatory stage of wound healing [51]. M1 macrophages, classified as classically activated macrophages, are considered to participate in promoting the inflammatory process, pathogen clearance, and Th1-type responses through the release of inflammatory cytokines such as TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β, IL-12, and iNOS (Fig. 4 a) [51–53]. However, the excessive release of pro-inflammatory factors has the potential to trigger local or systemic inflammation, which may result in damage to surrounding normal tissues [54]. M2 macrophages, classified as alternatively activated macrophages, release anti-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-10, TGF-β1, and arginase-1 (Arg-1) to suppress inflammation (Fig. 4a) [55–57]. They also contribute to angiogenesis, extracellular matrix (ECM) repair, and resolution of inflammation [58,59]. It is widely recognized that sustained or excessive inflammation can result in the persistent existence of chronic non-healing wounds, preventing wounds from entering the remodeling stage and consequently delaying the healing process [51,52,60]. Therefore, early inflammation development restriction and immune responses modulation after skin injury is essential for subsequent wound repair.

Herein, CPDs synthesized from quercetin aim to preserve its potent anti-inflammatory properties. An in vitro inflammatory model established by lipopolysaccharide (LPS) was then used to explore the immunoregulatory effects of Qu-CDs through macrophage polarization (Fig. 4a). As shown in Fig. 4b and c, untreated macrophages exhibit a non-polarized status with relatively low expression levels of both anti-inflammatory and pro-inflammatory factors. When exposed to LPS, macrophages transitioned to an M1 phenotype, detected by increased pro-inflammatory factors (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β). Treatment with Qu-CDs at increasing concentrations significantly suppressed these pro-inflammatory factors while promoting anti-inflammatory factors (IL-10, Arg-1), with stronger effects at higher concentrations (Fig. 4b and c). This transition of macrophages from M1 to M2 phenotypes is essential for suppressing excessive inflammatory response [61]. Quercetin (5 μg mL⁻1) also exhibited anti-inflammatory effects, albeit weaker than high-concentration Qu-CDs (125 μg mL−1). Immunofluorescence staining for M1 and M2 markers (iNOS for M1, CD206 for M2) confirmed that both Qu-CDs and quercetin promoted the M1 to M2 transition (Fig. 4d–g). Notably, the anti-inflammatory effect of Qu-CDs (125 μg mL−1) was more potent than quercetin at safe doses (5 μg mL⁻1) . These findings supported that, in comparison with quercetin, Qu-CDs exhibited superior immunoregulatory activities.

The anti-inflammatory characteristics exhibited by quercetin in this experiment were consistent with previous studies [45,62,63]. Quercetin might regulate the expression of inflammatory factors by modulating signaling pathways such as TGF-β, Wnt/β-catenin, and nuclear factor kappa B (NF-κB), thereby affecting the inflammatory process [45]. Yet, the poor water solubility and relatively high cytotoxicity of quercetin have restricted its practical use in treating various diseases. As Qu-CDs were covered with abundant preserved functional moieties of the quercetin precursor, they potentially inherited the anti-inflammatory properties of quercetin [64–67]. Moreover, the high water dispersibility and nanoscale dimensions of Qu-CDs could help to boost the anti-inflammatory activity. Additionally, the modulation of inflammatory responses by Qu-CDs could be associated with the NF-κB signaling pathway. The NF-κB pathway has been widely acknowledged as a pro-inflammatory signaling pathway, primarily regulating the expression of genes involved in inflammation, such as cytokines, chemokines, and adhesion molecules [68]. During the inflammatory phase of wound healing, NF-κB enhances the inflammatory response by upregulating the expression of pro-inflammatory factors such as TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β [69]. P65 serves as a critical positive regulator of the NF-κB family [70]. The activation of NF-κB is also a crucial factor in inducing macrophage differentiation into the M1 phenotype [71,72]. We thus investigated changes in p65 expression in macrophages treated with Qu-CDs (Fig. S17). LPS stimulation significantly increased phospho-p65 expression in macrophages, indicating activation of the NF-κB pathway under inflammatory conditions. However, treatment with Qu-CDs effectively suppressed LPS-induced phospho-p65 expression (Fig. S17). These findings suggested that Qu-CDs modulated macrophage phenotypic transitions by suppressing the NF-κB signaling pathway, ultimately mitigating excessive inflammatory responses. Burn wounds differ significantly from incisional wounds due to a pronounced inflammatory response, burn-induced pain, and immediate damage to blood flow at the injury site, collectively impairing healing and increasing the risk of scar formation [73–75]. In deep burns affecting the dermal reticular layer, re-epithelialization progresses slowly, relying on residual epidermal cells in hair follicles, while necrotic tissue obstructs vascularization and matrix deposition. Accumulated macrophages trigger excessive inflammation, disrupt the M1-to-M2 transition, and impair the functions of immune, endothelial, and epithelial cells, further hindering re-epithelialization and prolonging healing [31,76]. In summary, the regulation of Qu-CDs on macrophage phenotypes contributed to maintaining the balance of immune-inflammatory responses during burn healing and accelerates the transition from the inflammatory to the proliferative phase.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of Qu-CDs on cell migration mediated by EMT
ce:section.ce:para: Cell migration forms the foundation of the re-epithelialization phase, as it is essential to achieve the responses of various cell types participating in tissue repair [18]. Epithelial cells migrate collectively to restore tissue integrity after injury [19]. To evaluate cell migration, HaCaT cells were scratched and co-cultured with varying concentrations of Qu-CDs and quercetin for 24 h. The results indicated that Qu-CDs significantly reduced the scratch area in a concentration-dependent manner, with a 70 % healed area at 125 μg mL−1 (Fig. 5 a, S18b). In contrast, quercetin (5 μg mL−1) showed no effect on cell migration. The effect of Qu-CDs on migration promoting was confirmed by the Transwell assay (Figs. S18a and c). These results highlighted the potential of Qu-CDs to enhance cell migration, unlike quercetin when administered at safe doses.

An attractive mechanism model of cell migration involves EMT, during which epithelial cells lose their adhesive characteristics and increase their mobility [23,77]. Specifically, in the process of wound healing after skin injury, the surrounding keratinocytes initiate partial EMT by losing their epithelial phenotype of cell polarity and intercellular adhesion, and downregulating the expression of E-cadherin (E-cad). They also exhibit a mesenchymal phenotype, including enhanced migration ability and the expression of vimentin (Vim), fibronectin (Fn) and N-cadherin (N-cad) [78,79]. To date, ample evidence has confirmed that the EMT in keratinocytes is closely linked to their motility and directly influences the re-epithelialization process [24,25]. We subsequently investigated the effects of Qu-CDs on diverse markers of the EMT process. After 24 h co-culture with Qu-CDs, the epithelial marker expressions including E-cad and cytokeratin (CK) of HaCaT cells decreased significantly, whereas mesenchymal markers (N-cad, Vim) increased concentration-dependently, unlike quercetin which showed comparable expressions to control (Fig. 5b). Western blot and immunofluorescence staining confirmed these findings, showing that increasing Qu-CDs concentrations inhibited epithelial markers and promoted mesenchymal proteins. The most pronounced EMT induction was observed at 125 μg/mL Qu-CDs (Fig. 5c and d). Immunofluorescence images visually depicted protein expressions at 125 μg/mL Qu-CDs and 5 μg/mL quercetin (Fig. 5e–h). These findings suggested that Qu-CDs induced the EMT process in keratinocytes by triggering the loss of adhesive epithelial phenotype and promoting the expression of mesenchymal markers, which coordinates the motion of the multicellular assembly [80,81].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activation of smad1/5 signaling contributes to EMT and cell motility induced by Qu-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To explore transcriptomic alterations upon Qu-CDs treatment, RNA-seq analysis was performed on HaCaT cells exposed to 125 μg mL−1 Qu-CDs for 24 h, with the PBS group as the control. Compared to controls, 111 differentially expressed genes (p < 0.05, fold change >1.5) were identified (Fig. 6 a). Gene Ontology (GO) analysis revealed down-regulated genes associated with keratinocyte differentiation, cornification, and cornified envelope (Fig. 6b and Fig. S19a), with key signaling pathways linked to the TGF-β pathway (Fig. 6c and Fig. S19b). Based on GO, KEGG enrichment, and DEG volcano plots, DNA-binding protein inhibitor (ID) emerged as a potential key gene, downstream of the BMP-smad1/5 pathway within the TGF-β family. BMPs work by attaching to a combination of type I and type II serine/threonine kinase receptors, triggering the phosphorylation of receptor-activated smad transcription factors (R-smads) [23,82]. These R-smads then translocate into the nucleus and regulate gene expression after linking up with smad4. Recent studies increasingly suggest that smad1/5, phosphorylated in response to BMPs, regulates EMT-related transcription factors like Snail, Slug, and Twist1, promoting the EMT phenotype [83–89]. IDs, as targets of smad1/5, are intricately linked to the regulation of cell differentiation, proliferation, and death [90]. Thus, our transcriptional profiles suggested that Qu-CDs might have induced the EMT phenotype by activating the BMP-smad1/5 pathway, thereby promoting cell motility.

Therefore, the activation of smad1/5-mediated pathway in HaCaT cells was confirmed using qRT-PCR, western blot, immunofluorescence, and scratch assay. Remarkably, Qu-CDs significantly upregulated IDs gene expression compared to PBS-treated group (Fig. 6d). Correspondingly, western blot and immunofluorescence images also revealed increased phosphorylated smad1/5 in Qu-CDs-exposed HaCaT cells (Fig. 6f–h). To further investigate the role of smad1/5 in Qu-CDs-induced EMT and motility, the smad1/5 inhibitor (LDN193189) was used to block this pathway. LDN193189 effectively inhibited smad1/5 activation, reducing p-smad1/5 levels and IDs expression in Qu-CDs-treated HaCaT cells (Fig. 6d and Fig. 6f-h). Importantly, LDN193189 significantly inhibited Qu-CDs-induced EMT phenotypic markers, decreasing N-cad and Vim while increasing E-cad and CK expression at both the transcriptional and protein levels (Fig. 6e–h). These findings suggested that Qu-CDs predominantly drove the cellular EMT process, while the supplementation of LDN19318 could suppress this trend. Additionally, scratch assays revealed that LDN193189 suppressed Qu-CDs-induced cell migration (Fig. 6i and Fig. S20). In summary, our findings suggested that Qu-CDs promoted EMT and cell migration via the smad1/5 pathway. Activation of smad1/5 is crucial for EMT induced by the TGF-β family [83] and synergistically regulates cell differentiation with other pathways [86,91,92].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Qu-CDs facilitate full-thickness cutaneous burn wound healing in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Minimizing wound necrosis and infection risk is a key focus in burn management [5]. To further assess the impact of Qu-CDs on wound healing, full-thickness skin burn injuries were established on mice backs (Fig. 7 a). A silicone ring was sutured around the wound to prevent contraction by the subcutaneous muscle layer, enhancing the correlation with human skin injury healing [7,93,94]. Saline, quercetin, EGF (commercial control), and Qu-CDs were sprayed topically every three days until wound closure. EGF is recognized for its ability to facilitate the repair of skin and mucosal wounds and is commonly used in clinical settings. Wound area measurements on days 3, 7, 14, and 21 exhibited that both EGF and Qu-CDs accelerated wound contraction and closure, especially at days 3, 7, and 14, whereas quercetin showed no significant effect (Fig. 7b and c, Fig. S21). Histological analysis by H&E staining confirmed enhanced recovery in the EGF and Qu-CDs groups, with evident epithelial migration and healing in the injured regions (Fig. 7d). At day 14, the Qu-CDs group exhibited new sebaceous glands. By day 21, wounds were completely closed, with Qu-CDs group closely resembling mature normal tissue in epidermal thickness, morphology, and appendage development, indicating successful re-epithelialization (Fig. 7d, Fig. S22). Mason's trichrome staining (Fig. S23) revealed higher collagen fiber deposition in the EGF and Qu-CDs groups by day 14 compared to the control and quercetin groups. On day 21, the EGF and Qu-CDs groups exhibited a more organized and continuous arrangement of collagen fibers. These findings demonstrated that Qu-CDs could promote faster re-epithelialization, earlier skin appendage regeneration, and more organized collagen fiber arrangement, achieving burn wound healing efficacy comparable to EGF.

To assess the regulation of inflammatory factor expression, tissues were collected on days 3 and 7 for immunofluorescence staining (Fig. 8 a). Both quercetin and Qu-CDs reduced TNF-α and IL-1β expression in wound tissues, with Qu-CDs appearing a more significant effect. This indicated favorable anti-inflammatory properties in vivo of Qu-CDs, accelerating the transition of wounds towards the repair stage. Afterwards, immunohistochemical staining was conducted to evaluate the EMT status (Fig. 8b and c). On days 7 and 14, a significant reduction in the expression of E-cad in epithelial cells was observed in the Qu-CDs group, concomitant with an increase in N-cad-positive mesenchymal cells, indicating the in vivo EMT induction by Qu-CDs. Additionally, p-smad1/5 expression during epithelial healing was detected (Fig. 8d, Fig. S24). In the Qu-CDs group, robust p-smad1/5 immunoreactivity was observed in epithelial cells at the wound.

Taken collectively, these observations aligned with the in vitro findings and verify that Qu-CDs have the potential to accelerate the transition of wounds to the repair phase by modulating immune responses, and to reinforce wound re-epithelialization by actively inducing the EMT process and epithelial cell migration via the smad1/5 signaling pathway, ultimately facilitating the reconstruction of the normal epidermal barrier and achieving rapid wound closure (Fig. 8e).

In conclusion, for the first time, the bioactive flavonol quercetin is transformed into pharmacologically active carbonized polymer dots (Qu-CDs) spray with high water dispersibility, permeability and biocompatibility for full-thickness skin burns treatment. Qu-CDs could effectively initiate macrophage reprogramming, modulating immune responses by inducing the transition of macrophages from M1 to M2 phenotype, thereby facilitating the shift of wounds from the inflammatory phase to the re-epithelialization phase. Importantly, Qu-CDs could facilitate cell migration and re-epithelialization of the wound edge cells by inducing EMT phenotype without any growth factors, and further contributing to the reconstruction of the normal epidermal barrier. Mechanistically, Qu-CDs could induce epithelial cells to initiate EMT phenotype by regulating the expression of the smad1/5 signaling pathway. Although Qu-CDs exhibit considerable promise in skin regeneration, several challenges remain for their practical application, including the suitability for varying depths of skin damage, evaluation of long-term healing effects and scar formation risks, and efficacy in treating infected or diabetic wounds. Collectively, these sprayable Qu-CDs provide a new paradigm to develop bioactive carbonized polymer dots with desired pharmacological activity to address the practical needs in burn wound healing.
